content.text: What Is a Security Principal

A security principal is an object that represents an identity requesting access to Azure resources. Every role assignment in Azure RBAC binds three things: a security principal (who), a role definition (what), and a scope (where). Understanding the four types of security principals and when to use each is the foundation of a well-designed RBAC model.

Azure recognizes four security principal types. Users and groups represent human identities managed in Microsoft Entra ID. Service principals represent application workloads — code that needs to call Azure APIs. Managed identities are a special form of service principal where Azure manages the credential lifecycle, eliminating the need to store or rotate secrets.
Users

A user is a human identity with a UPN (user@domain.com) in your Entra ID tenant, or a guest user invited from another tenant or a personal Microsoft account. Users are the most granular assignment target — assign roles directly when access is one-off or unique to a single person, but prefer groups for any repeating pattern.
Member users belong to your tenant's own directory. Guest users are external identities added via B2B collaboration.
The user's Object ID (a GUID) is the stable identifier used in role assignments — the UPN can be renamed without breaking assignments.
Entra ID Privileged Identity Management (PIM) can make role assignments for users time-bound and require activation approval.
Guest users can be assigned roles, but some built-in roles restrict guest access by default.

# List all users in the tenant
Get-MgUser -All | Select-Object DisplayName, UserPrincipalName, AccountEnabled | Format-Table

# Get a specific user's object ID (needed for role assignments)
$user = Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'"
$user.Id   # This is the security principal ID used in role assignments

# Assign a role to a user at resource group scope
$rg = Get-AzResourceGroup -Name 'prod-rg'
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $user.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope $rg.ResourceId

# List role assignments for a specific user (including inherited)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $user.Id | Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope

# Check effective permissions at a specific resource
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $user.Id -Scope $rg.ResourceId
Groups

Assigning roles to groups rather than individual users is the recommended practice for any team or workload. When a user joins or leaves the team, you update group membership — the role assignment itself does not change. This dramatically reduces the operational surface of RBAC management.
Use security groups (not Microsoft 365 groups) when the sole purpose is RBAC. M365 groups work but carry mail/Teams overhead.
Dynamic membership rules auto-update group membership when user attributes (department, jobTitle, location) change in Entra ID.
Nested group membership is honored: members of a nested group inherit the role assignment of the parent group.
Groups cannot be assigned to groups in Entra ID B2B scenarios — only direct members are evaluated in RBAC.

# Create a security group for RBAC
$group = New-MgGroup -DisplayName 'DevOps Engineers' `
    -MailEnabled:$false `
    -SecurityEnabled:$true `
    -MailNickname 'devops-engineers'

# Add a member to the group
New-MgGroupMember -GroupId $group.Id `
    -DirectoryObjectId $user.Id

# Assign a role to the group at subscription scope
$subId = (Get-AzSubscription).Id
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $group.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
  -Scope "/subscriptions/$subId"

# Nested groups: members of nested groups DO inherit role assignments
# Note: Microsoft 365 Groups (mail-enabled) are supported for RBAC in Entra ID
# Dynamic groups: membership auto-updates when user attributes change
$dynGroup = New-MgGroup -DisplayName 'All Developers' `
    -MailEnabled:$false -SecurityEnabled:$true `
    -MailNickname 'all-developers' `
    -MembershipRule '(user.department -eq "Engineering")' `
    -MembershipRuleProcessingState 'On' `
    -GroupTypes @('DynamicMembership')

# View group membership (direct members only)
Get-MgGroupMember -GroupId $group.Id | Select-Object Id, @{N='Type';E={$_.AdditionalProperties['@odata.type']}}
Service Principals

A service principal is the tenant-local instance of an application registration. When you register an app in Entra ID, a service principal is created in your tenant. You then grant the service principal RBAC roles it needs to act on Azure resources on behalf of the application. CI/CD pipelines, background services, and cross-service integrations typically authenticate as a service principal using a client secret or certificate.
Every app registration in a tenant has exactly one service principal in that tenant. Multi-tenant apps get one SP per tenant they are used in.
Client secrets and certificates are credentials attached to the app registration, not the service principal. Both share the same client ID.
Prefer certificates over client secrets where possible — certificates are harder to exfiltrate than short string secrets.
Secrets expire; automate rotation with Key Vault references and track expiry dates to avoid outages.
Service principals created by Azure services (e.g., Azure DevOps service connections) are managed externally — do not delete their SP without checking the service.

# A Service Principal is automatically created when an app is registered
# or when an Azure Marketplace app is added to your tenant

# Get the SP for a registered app
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "DisplayName eq 'MyPipelineApp'"
$sp.Id   # Use this ObjectId for role assignments

# Create an app registration + its service principal
$app = New-MgApplication -DisplayName 'MyPipelineApp'
$sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $app.AppId

# Assign a role to the service principal
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/prod-rg'

# Create a client secret for the SP (rotate on a schedule)
$cred = Add-MgApplicationPassword -ApplicationId $app.Id `
    -PasswordCredential @{ DisplayName='CI secret'; EndDateTime=(Get-Date).AddYears(1) }
$cred.SecretText   # Only shown once — store in Key Vault immediately

# List all service principals with expiring credentials (next 30 days)
$cutoff = (Get-Date).AddDays(30)
Get-MgServicePrincipal -All | Where-Object {
    $_.PasswordCredentials | Where-Object { $_.EndDateTime -lt $cutoff }
} | Select-Object DisplayName, AppId
Managed Identities

Managed identities are the preferred mechanism for Azure-hosted workloads that need to access other Azure services. Azure automatically provisions and rotates the underlying credential — you never see a secret. The application code requests an access token from the Azure Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint and presents it to the target service, with no configuration of secrets.
Type
Lifecycle
Shared Across Resources?
Best For
System-Assigned
Tied to the Azure resource; deleted with it
No — one identity per resource
Single-purpose VMs, Functions, App Services
User-Assigned
Managed independently as its own Azure resource
Yes — attach to multiple resources
Shared identity for a workload spanning multiple Azure resources

# System-Assigned Managed Identity — tied to a specific resource
# Enable on an existing VM
Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' -Name 'app-vm01' `
    -IdentityType SystemAssigned

# Get the identity's Principal ID (object ID in Entra ID)
$vm = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' -Name 'app-vm01'
$principalId = $vm.Identity.PrincipalId

# Assign a role to the system-assigned identity
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $principalId `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/prod-kv'

# User-Assigned Managed Identity — reusable across multiple resources
$umi = New-AzUserAssignedIdentity -ResourceGroupName 'identity-rg' `
    -Name 'app-worker-identity' -Location 'eastus'

# Assign the UMI to a VM
Update-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'prod-rg' -Name 'app-vm02' `
    -IdentityType UserAssigned `
    -IdentityId $umi.Id

# Grant the UMI access to Key Vault
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $umi.PrincipalId `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/prod-kv'

# In application code, get a token using the managed identity (no secret needed)
# Python example using azure-identity:
# from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
# from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
# cred = ManagedIdentityCredential()  # or ManagedIdentityCredential(client_id='<umi-client-id>')
# client = SecretClient(vault_url='https://prod-kv.vault.azure.net', credential=cred)
# secret = client.get_secret('db-password')

Managed identities are available on VMs, App Service, Functions, AKS (pod identity/workload identity), Container Instances, Logic Apps, Service Bus, and many other Azure services. Always prefer managed identity over a service principal + secret for Azure-to-Azure authentication.
Choosing the Right Principal Type
Picking the wrong principal type adds unnecessary complexity or risk. Use this decision guide:
Scenario
Recommended Principal
Reason
Individual developer needs access to a resource group
User (directly or via group)
Human identity, tracked by name
Team of 5 developers all need the same access
Group
Manage membership once, not 5 role assignments
CI/CD pipeline deploys to Azure from GitHub Actions
Service Principal + federated credential (OIDC)
No stored secret; GitHub OIDC token is exchanged at runtime
Azure Function reads secrets from Key Vault
Managed Identity (system-assigned)
No secret to store or rotate
Fleet of 10 VMs share the same Key Vault access
Managed Identity (user-assigned)
One identity, one role assignment, attached to all 10 VMs
Third-party SaaS needs read access to your storage
Service Principal (app registration)
External party controls the app; SP is in your tenant
External consultant needs temporary access
Guest user + PIM eligible assignment
Time-limited, audited, requires activation
Auditing and Governance

Role assignments should be reviewed regularly. Orphaned assignments (ObjectType = Unknown) accumulate over time as users are deleted and apps are decommissioned. These assignments grant no access but obscure the true state of RBAC and should be cleaned up.
Run quarterly access reviews in Entra ID Access Reviews to have resource owners attest to group memberships and role assignments.
Service principal assignments with no matching app registration indicate orphaned or deleted apps — investigate before removing.
Managed identity assignments are scoped to specific resources; review them when the Azure resource is decommissioned.
Use Azure Policy to enforce that certain role assignments can only target specific principal types — for example, requiring managed identities for compute workloads.

# List all role assignments in a subscription, showing principal type
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName,
    ObjectType, Scope | Sort-Object Scope | Format-Table -AutoSize

# Find role assignments for all service principals (automated workloads)
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq 'ServicePrincipal' } |
    Select-Object DisplayName, RoleDefinitionName, Scope

# Detect 'orphaned' role assignments (principal deleted, Unknown type)
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq 'Unknown' } |
    Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, ObjectId, Scope

# Export all assignments to CSV for governance review
Get-AzRoleAssignment | Export-Csv rbac-audit.csv -NoTypeInformation

# Using Azure CLI for cross-subscription reporting
az role assignment list --all --include-inherited `
    --query '[].{Principal:principalName,Type:principalType,Role:roleDefinitionName,Scope:scope}' `
    --output table
Federated Identity Credentials for Service Principals

Workload identity federation lets an external identity provider (GitHub Actions, Kubernetes, GitLab, Terraform Cloud) exchange its own OIDC token for an Entra ID access token. The service principal has no stored password or certificate — the trust relationship itself is the credential. This is the recommended approach for all CI/CD pipelines and Kubernetes workloads.
The federated credential specifies the issuer URL (the external OIDC provider) and a subject claim (which workflow, branch, or pod SA is trusted).
GitHub Actions: set subject to repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main to trust only the main branch; use environment:production for environment-gated deployments.
AKS Workload Identity: the OIDC issuer is unique per cluster; the subject is system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<sa-name>.
No secrets to rotate, no secrets to leak. The GitHub OIDC token is short-lived (15 minutes) and issued fresh each workflow run.

# Workload identity federation allows external identity providers (GitHub, Kubernetes)
# to authenticate as a service principal WITHOUT a stored secret.
# The external OIDC token is presented to Entra ID in exchange for an access token.

# Step 1: Create the app registration (if not already done)
$app = New-MgApplication -DisplayName 'github-actions-prod'
$sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $app.AppId

# Step 2: Add a federated credential for GitHub Actions
# This trusts tokens issued by GitHub for a specific repo and branch
$fedCred = @{
    name = 'github-prod-deploy'
    issuer = 'https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com'
    subject = 'repo:contoso/myapp:ref:refs/heads/main'
    audiences = @('api://AzureADTokenExchange')
    description = 'GitHub Actions deployment from main branch'
}
New-MgApplicationFederatedIdentityCredential -ApplicationId $app.Id -BodyParameter $fedCred

# Step 3: In GitHub Actions, authenticate using OIDC (no secrets in GitHub)
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
# permissions:
#   id-token: write
#   contents: read
# - uses: azure/login@v1
#   with:
#     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
#     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
#     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

# Step 4: Grant RBAC role to the SP
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/prod-rg'

# List existing federated credentials for an app
Get-MgApplicationFederatedIdentityCredential -ApplicationId $app.Id |
    Select-Object Name, Issuer, Subject, Audiences

# Kubernetes workload identity federation (AKS pod authentication)
# The Kubernetes SA token is exchanged for an Entra access token
$k8sCred = @{
    name = 'aks-workload-identity'
    issuer = 'https://oidc.prod-aks-eastus.azure.com/<cluster-id>/'
    subject = 'system:serviceaccount:default:app-sa'
    audiences = @('api://AzureADTokenExchange')
}
New-MgApplicationFederatedIdentityCredential -ApplicationId $app.Id -BodyParameter $k8sCred

Federated credentials are the gold standard for CI/CD authentication. If your pipeline currently uses a stored client secret, migrating to federated credentials eliminates an entire class of supply-chain risk at no operational cost.
Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for Security Principals

Entra ID Privileged Identity Management (PIM) adds just-in-time activation to role assignments. Instead of a user permanently holding an active Contributor role, they hold an eligible assignment and activate it for a limited window (e.g., 8 hours) with an MFA challenge and optional approval. PIM works for both Entra directory roles and Azure RBAC resource roles.
Assignment State
Behavior
When to Use
Active (permanent)
Always has the role; no activation needed
Break-glass accounts, service principals
Eligible (PIM)
Must activate; MFA + justification required
Admins, privileged operations, audited tasks
Time-bound Active
Active only during a specified window
Temporary project access, contractors

# Entra ID PIM (P2 license required) makes role assignments eligible rather than active.
# Users activate their eligible assignments on-demand, with optional approval and MFA.

# Install the PIM PowerShell module
Import-Module Microsoft.Graph.Identity.Governance

# List all eligible role assignments for a user
$userId = (Get-MgUser -Filter "UserPrincipalName eq 'alice@contoso.com'").Id
Get-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleEligibilityScheduleInstance `
    -Filter "principalId eq '$userId'"

# Create an eligible assignment (requires PIM)
# The user must activate this role; it is not active by default
$params = @{
    action = 'adminAssign'
    justification = 'Finance team RBAC reviewer'
    roleDefinitionId = 'acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7'  # Reader
    directoryScopeId = '/'
    principalId = $userId
    scheduleInfo = @{
        startDateTime = (Get-Date -Format 'o')
        expiration = @{ type = 'NoExpiration' }
    }
}
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleEligibilityScheduleRequest -BodyParameter $params

# Activate an eligible assignment (user self-service)
$activateParams = @{
    action = 'selfActivate'
    justification = 'Quarterly RBAC access review'
    roleDefinitionId = 'acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7'
    directoryScopeId = '/'
    principalId = $userId
    scheduleInfo = @{
        startDateTime = (Get-Date -Format 'o')
        expiration = @{ type = 'AfterDuration'; duration = 'PT8H' }  # 8 hours
    }
}
New-MgRoleManagementDirectoryRoleAssignmentScheduleRequest -BodyParameter $activateParams

# PIM for Azure resources (subscription / resource group / resource scope)
# View active assignments at subscription scope
Get-AzRoleAssignmentScheduleInstance -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>' |
    Where-Object { $_.AssignmentType -eq 'Activated' } |
    Select-Object PrincipalName, RoleDefinitionName, StartDateTime, EndDateTime
Cross-Tenant and Delegated Access

When partner organizations, managed service providers, or multi-tenant applications need access to your Azure resources, there are two patterns: invite the external service principal as a B2B guest into your tenant, or use Azure Lighthouse for long-term delegated management access. Both result in a role assignment in your tenant, but Lighthouse does not require the external identity to be replicated into your directory.

# Cross-tenant service principal access (B2B scenario)
# When a partner organization's app needs access to your resources:

# Option 1: Invite the app's service principal as a guest
# (The external SP must exist in its home tenant first)
$externalSpAppId = '<partner-app-client-id>'
$invitation = @{
    invitedUserEmailAddress = 'partner-app@partnertenant.onmicrosoft.com'
    inviteRedirectUrl = 'https://portal.azure.com'
    sendInvitationMessage = $false
}
# After invitation, get the guest's local object ID
$guestSp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "AppId eq '$externalSpAppId'"
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $guestSp.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Reader' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/shared-rg'

# Option 2: Azure Lighthouse (preferred for managed service providers)
# Delegated resource management: the partner's principals get roles in your subscription
# without needing to be in your Entra tenant at all
# Deploy a Lighthouse authorization via ARM template:
@"
{
  "authorizations": [
    {
      "principalId": "<partner-user-or-group-object-id>",
      "roleDefinitionId": "acdd72a7-3385-48ef-bd42-f606fba81ae7",
      "principalIdDisplayName": "Partner NOC Team"
    }
  ]
}
"@

# List all Lighthouse delegations on your subscription
Get-AzManagedServicesDefinition | Select-Object Name, Description
Get-AzManagedServicesAssignment | Select-Object Name, ProvisioningState
Pattern
External SP in Your Tenant?
Governance Model
Best For
B2B Guest SP
Yes — appears as a guest object
You manage the guest SP's role assignments
One-off integration with known partner app
Azure Lighthouse
No — stays in partner tenant
Partner manages their own users; you approve the delegation
MSP / ongoing delegated management at scale
Summary
Azure RBAC security principals are the 'who' in every role assignment. Each type serves a distinct purpose:
Users — human identities; assign directly only for unique cases, otherwise use groups.
Groups — the standard pattern for teams; dynamic groups keep membership in sync with user attributes automatically.
Service Principals — application identities; use certificates or federated credentials instead of secrets where possible.
Managed Identities — the best choice for Azure-hosted workloads; Azure owns the credential lifecycle, eliminating secret management entirely.
Audit role assignments regularly to remove orphaned entries and enforce least privilege.
